---
title: "Primers for AD698 - Applied Generative AI for Analytics"
subtitle: "Primer 5 - Similarity and Semantic Search"
author: "Nakul R. Padalkar"
institute: "Boston University"
number-sections: false
date: "2026-01-01"
date-modified: today
date-format: long
engine: jupyter
bibliography: ../../references.bib
categories: ["M0", "Primer", "Similarity", "Semantic Search", "Foundations"]
description: "A technical primer explaining similarity metrics, nearest-neighbor retrieval, hybrid search and evaluation for semantic search systems."
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    fontsize: 10pt
    css: primer-style.css
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Similarity and Semantic Search

Search is one of the core operations in generative AI systems. Before a model
can answer a question about a policy, contract, research memo, support ticket or
knowledge base, the system must decide which pieces of evidence are relevant.
Semantic search uses learned representations to retrieve material that is
meaningfully related to a query, even when the exact words differ.

Traditional keyword search asks whether query terms occur in a document.
Semantic search asks whether the query and document occupy nearby regions in an
embedding space. In modern retrieval-augmented generation, both signals often
matter.
The lineage runs from vector-space retrieval and probabilistic IR to modern
dense retrieval and RAG [@Salton.Wong.Yang.1975;
@ManningRaghavanSchutze2008IR; @Lewis2020RAG].

::: {.key-box}
**Key idea.** Semantic search converts retrieval into a nearest-neighbor
problem: embed the query, embed the documents, compare vectors, then return the
documents most similar to the query under the chosen metric.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-semantic-search-panels
#| fig-cap: "Three views of semantic search. (a) Keyword retrieval can miss paraphrases while semantic retrieval can find related language. (b) Ranking depends on the similarity metric and threshold. (c) Hybrid retrieval combines lexical and embedding-based evidence before reranking."
#| fig-width: 8.2
#| fig-height: 2.75
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.rcParams.update({
    "font.size": 8,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "figure.dpi": 140
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(8.2, 2.75))

# Panel a: keyword vs semantic matches
ax = axes[0]
ax.axis("off")
ax.text(0.02, 0.93, "a", fontweight="bold", transform=ax.transAxes)
ax.set_title("Exact terms and paraphrases", pad=4)
rows = [
    ("query", "renewal delayed by security review", "#111827"),
    ("keyword hit", "security review delayed renewal", "#2563eb"),
    ("semantic hit", "customer waiting on infosec approval", "#047857"),
    ("miss", "office lease renewal deadline", "#9ca3af"),
]
for i, (label, text, color) in enumerate(rows):
    y = 0.76 - i * 0.18
    ax.text(0.04, y, label, color=color, fontweight="bold", fontsize=8)
    ax.text(0.34, y, text, color=color, fontsize=8)
    ax.plot([0.32, 0.32], [0.16, 0.84], color="#e5e7eb", lw=0.8)

# Panel b: similarity threshold
ax = axes[1]
docs = np.arange(1, 9)
scores = np.array([0.91, 0.84, 0.76, 0.63, 0.55, 0.42, 0.36, 0.22])
colors = ["#047857" if s >= 0.60 else "#9ca3af" for s in scores]
ax.bar(docs, scores, color=colors)
ax.axhline(0.60, color="#111827", lw=1.0, ls="--")
ax.text(6.0, 0.63, "threshold", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.0)
ax.set_xlabel("candidate document")
ax.set_ylabel("similarity")
ax.set_title("Ranking requires a cutoff", pad=4)
ax.text(0.55, 0.94, "b", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xticks(docs)

# Panel c: hybrid retrieval
ax = axes[2]
ax.axis("off")
ax.text(0.02, 0.93, "c", fontweight="bold", transform=ax.transAxes)
ax.set_title("Hybrid retrieval pipeline", pad=4)
boxes = [
    (0.06, 0.66, "query"),
    (0.36, 0.78, "keyword\nsearch"),
    (0.36, 0.54, "vector\nsearch"),
    (0.66, 0.66, "rerank"),
    (0.66, 0.34, "top-k\nevidence"),
]
for x, y, label in boxes:
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x, y), 0.22, 0.13, fill=False, ec="#111827", lw=1.0))
    ax.text(x + 0.11, y + 0.065, label, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8)
for start, end in [
    ((0.28, 0.72), (0.36, 0.84)),
    ((0.28, 0.72), (0.36, 0.60)),
    ((0.58, 0.84), (0.66, 0.73)),
    ((0.58, 0.60), (0.66, 0.70)),
    ((0.77, 0.66), (0.77, 0.47)),
]:
    ax.annotate("", xy=end, xytext=start, arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", lw=0.9))

fig.tight_layout(w_pad=1.0)
plt.show()
```

**Figure 1a** shows the central motivation for semantic search. Exact keyword
retrieval works well when the document uses the same words as the query. It can
miss relevant paraphrases, such as "infosec approval" for "security review."
Semantic retrieval can recover such matches because similarity is computed in a
learned vector space rather than only through exact term overlap.

**Figure 1b** shows that retrieval is not only a scoring problem. It is also a
selection problem. The system must choose how many documents to return or what
similarity threshold to apply. A threshold that is too strict may omit necessary
evidence; a threshold that is too loose may pass irrelevant material into the
generation step.

**Figure 1c** shows a common production pattern. Hybrid retrieval combines
keyword search and vector search, then reranks the combined candidates. This is
often stronger than either method alone because business documents contain both
semantic meaning and exact identifiers such as product names, clause numbers,
invoice IDs, dates and policy codes.

# Metrics of Similarity

Embedding systems usually compare vectors with a distance or similarity metric.
The most common are cosine similarity, dot product and Euclidean distance.

Cosine similarity compares direction:

$$
\begin{align}
\text{cosine}(a,b)
  &= \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}.
\end{align}
$$

Dot product compares both direction and magnitude:

$$
\begin{align}
a \cdot b
  &= \sum_i a_i b_i.
\end{align}
$$

Euclidean distance compares geometric distance:

$$
\begin{align}
d(a,b)
  &= \sqrt{\sum_i (a_i-b_i)^2}.
\end{align}
$$

In many text-embedding workflows, vectors are normalized, making cosine
similarity and dot product closely related. The correct metric depends on how
the embedding model was trained and how the vector index is configured.

Metrics also encode assumptions. Cosine similarity ignores vector magnitude
after normalization, which is useful when direction carries the semantic signal.
Euclidean distance treats absolute position as meaningful. Dot product can
reward both direction and magnitude. A search system should use the metric
recommended for the embedding model rather than treating metrics as
interchangeable knobs.

# Retrieval Is Approximate

Large document collections cannot always compare a query vector to every stored
vector exactly. Vector databases often use approximate nearest-neighbor indexes
to trade a small amount of retrieval accuracy for speed. This creates practical
design parameters:

- index type;
- recall target;
- latency budget;
- number of candidates;
- metadata filters;
- reranking model;
- similarity threshold.

The engineering question is not simply "does search work?" It is whether the
right evidence is retrieved quickly enough, cheaply enough and consistently
enough for the business decision being supported.

Approximation creates an evaluation obligation. If the index misses relevant
neighbors, the language model may never see the evidence required to answer
correctly. Increasing the candidate pool can improve recall but also increases
reranking cost and may pass more distracting evidence downstream.

# Semantic Search in RAG

In retrieval-augmented generation, semantic search is upstream of generation.
If the retrieved evidence is wrong, the model may produce an answer that is
fluent but unsupported. A strong answer requires both retrieval quality and
generation quality.

A typical RAG retrieval stage is:

1. accept a user query;
2. rewrite or expand the query if needed;
3. embed the query;
4. retrieve nearest document chunks;
5. filter by metadata;
6. rerank candidates;
7. pass selected evidence to the language model.

Each step changes what the model can know. Query rewriting may clarify intent.
Metadata filtering may remove documents from the wrong department or time
period. Reranking may move the most answer-bearing passage into the top few
results.

RAG is therefore not a single technique but a chain of retrieval decisions.
Chunking determines candidate units, embeddings determine neighborhoods,
metadata filters determine eligibility, rerankers determine ordering, and the
prompt determines how evidence is presented to the generator.

::: {.pitfall-box}
**Pitfall.** A high similarity score does not guarantee answerability. A
retrieved passage can be topically related to the query without containing the
specific evidence needed to answer it.
:::

# Evaluation

Search systems should be evaluated before they are connected to generation.
Useful retrieval metrics include:

| Metric | Meaning |
|---|---|
| recall@k | whether a relevant passage appears in the top \(k\) results |
| precision@k | how many of the top \(k\) results are relevant |
| MRR | how high the first relevant result appears |
| nDCG | whether highly relevant results appear near the top |
| answerability | whether retrieved evidence is sufficient to answer |

For business uses, answerability is especially important. A retrieved
chunk may be semantically similar and still fail to contain the needed number,
date, source, condition or exception.

# Demonstration: Related Is Not Sufficient

Suppose a manager asks:

> Why are enterprise renewals delayed this quarter?

Keyword retrieval might find documents containing `renewal` and `delayed`.
Semantic retrieval might also find `procurement hold`, `security approval`,
`legal redlines`, `data processing addendum`, and `budget freeze`. A hybrid
system can combine both. A reranker can then prioritize passages that actually
explain renewal delay rather than merely mention renewal.

The final answer should cite or trace back to the retrieved evidence. Otherwise
the language model may merge real evidence with plausible general business
explanations.

The retrieval system should therefore be tested with adversarial near misses:
documents that mention the same topic but do not answer the question. These
near misses are more informative than obviously irrelevant documents because
they reveal whether the system can distinguish topicality from answerability.

::: {.takeaway-box}
**Takeaway.** Semantic search is not just vector similarity. It is a retrieval
system with metrics, thresholds, filtering, reranking and evaluation. In GenAI
applications, retrieval quality sets the ceiling for grounded generation.
:::

### References

For classic retrieval foundations, see Salton et al. on vector-space retrieval
[@Salton.Wong.Yang.1975] and Manning, Raghavan and Schuetze on information
retrieval [@ManningRaghavanSchutze2008IR]. For modern RAG, see Lewis et al.
[@Lewis2020RAG] and the information-retrieval/RAG chapter in Jurafsky and
Martin [@JurafskyMartin2026SLP].
